inbound.subject: Starting over after a layoff
inbound.body: Hi coach, I want to get back to running after a messy few months. My big goal is to get healthy enough to race a half marathon again this spring. Right now I mostly need help rebuilding without doing something stupid.

outbound.subject: Re: Starting over after a layoff
outbound.text: You want to rebuild safely after a messy few months so you can race a half marathon this spring — I hear you. To get started I only need one thing: your time availability. Tell me how many days per week you can realistically train and either a typical session length (minutes) or a total weekly time budget. Once I have that, I’ll put together the first cautious, gradual rebuild outline and send it over.
outbound.html: <html><body>You want to rebuild safely after a messy few months so you can race a half marathon this spring — I hear you. To get started I only need one thing: your time availability. Tell me how many days per week you can realistically train and either a typical session length (minutes) or a total weekly time budget. Once I have that, I’ll put together the first cautious, gradual rebuild outline and send it over.</body></html>

inbound.subject: A little more context
inbound.body: A bit more context: I used to run consistently before the layoff and I'd call myself intermediate. I can train four days a week most weeks if we keep it realistic. The thing that nags me is mild Achilles tightness when I get greedy. I also do better when the plan feels structured.

outbound.subject: Re: A little more context
outbound.text: Great — I have everything I need to start building your plan. I'll have your first week ready for you shortly. Just reply to this email anytime you want to check in.
outbound.html: <html><body>Great — I have everything I need to start building your plan. I'll have your first week ready for you shortly. Just reply to this email anytime you want to check in.</body></html>

inbound.subject: Profile details and target race
inbound.body: Here is the missing context more directly. Primary goal: run a half marathon on 2026-05-17 and finish feeling strong. Time availability: 4 sessions per week and about 4 to 5 hours total. Experience level: intermediate. Constraints: mild Achilles tightness, busy work mornings on Tuesdays, and I prefer structured guidance.

outbound.subject: Re: Profile details and target race
outbound.text:
You're aiming for a spring half marathon and you’re managing mild Achilles tightness. We’ll keep this week conservative and consistent to protect the tendon while building readiness. This four-session plan fits your 4–5 hour window and prioritizes energy management and steady progress.
Session plan for the week (4 sessions, ~4–5 hours total)
- Session 1 — Easy Aerobic, 40–50 minutes
- Structure: 5–7 minutes easy warm-up, 30–40 minutes easy aerobic run at a conversational pace, 5–7 minutes cooldown including light calf/ankle mobility
- Focus: light cadence, tall posture, no pace targets; avoid pain
- Session 2 — Easy Aerobic, 40–50 minutes
- Structure: same as Session 1
- Focus: again, keep effort very gentle and controlled; if energy allows, you may finish with a short 5–10 minute mobility cooldown
- Session 3 — Reduced Intensity or Easy, 30–40 minutes
- Structure: easy pace throughout; no hard efforts
- Focus: keep it comfortable; this is a maintenance/this week’s hinge toward consistent loading
- Session 4 — Strength, 40–50 minutes
- Focus: Achilles-friendly strength to support running
- Suggested sequence (2–3 sets of 8–12 reps, light loads):
- Calf raises with controlled lowering (eccentric emphasis)
- Glute bridges or hip thrusts
- Split squats or elevated step-ups on a low height
- Core work (planks and side planks)
- Gentle ankle mobility work and hip mobility
Scheduling note
- Tue mornings are busy for you, so these sessions should avoid early-day slots on Tuesdays. A practical cadence could be Mon, Wed, Thu/Fri, and Sat, with adjustments as needed to fit your week. All four sessions stay easy and conservative given the current Achilles tightness and yellow risk posture.
Key safety and focus for the week
- Achilles care: daily calf/ankle mobility; keep calf loading gentle, avoid painful ranges; finish runs with a brief mobility cooldown
- Energy management: energy is the limiting signal—keep each session easy and short if needed; if energy dips, cut short; if you rebound, resume gradually instead of trying to “make up” volume
- Rest and recovery: prioritize sleep, steady nutrition, and downshifting stress to support adaptation
This plan keeps you moving, protects your Achilles, and builds toward a healthy half marathon in spring. Let me know how it goes.

inbound.subject: Availability update for this week
inbound.body: Availability update for this week. Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 4. Pain score: 2 out of 10. Risk candidate feels green. I can run Monday, Wednesday, Friday, and Sunday. Week is not chaotic. Schedule variability is medium because work may move one session by a few hours.

outbound.subject: Re: Availability update for this week
outbound.text:
Availability confirmed for four sessions this week on Mon, Wed, Fri, and Sun. Pain is mild (2/10) and the risk remains green. The plan stays conservative and focused on building toward your May 17 half marathon while respecting your current workload and Achilles protection.
Session 1 — Monday: Easy Aerobic 40–50 min (include warm-up, cooldown, and mobility). Cadence light; posture tall.
Session 2 — Wednesday: Tempo 40–50 min total. 10 min easy warm-up, 15–25 min at a controlled tempo, 5–10 min easy cooldown. Keep effort steady and controlled.
Session 3 — Friday: Easy Aerobic 40–50 min.
Session 4 — Sunday: Achilles-friendly Strength 40–50 min. Calf raises eccentrics, glute work, split squats, core, and ankle mobility. Use light-to-moderate loads and avoid painful calf loading.
Key guardrails:
- If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade the session to easy. Do not try to “make up” missed intensity later in the week.
- Prioritize sleep and simple recovery habits to support load tolerance.
- Maintain daily calf/ankle mobility and keep runs very easy with short duration if energy feels limited.
Flexibility:
- Your week can shift by a few hours without changing the overall plan. If a session needs to move, aim to keep the same order and load.
Optional sign-off:
Let me know how it goes.
outbound.html:

Session 1 — Monday: Easy Aerobic 40–50 min (include warm-up, cooldown, and mobility). Cadence light; posture tall.
Session 2 — Wednesday: Tempo 40–50 min total. 10 min easy warm-up, 15–25 min at a controlled tempo, 5–10 min easy cooldown. Keep effort steady and controlled.
Session 3 — Friday: Easy Aerobic 40–50 min.
Session 4 — Sunday: Achilles-friendly Strength 40–50 min. Calf raises eccentrics, glute work, split squats, core, and ankle mobility. Use light-to-moderate loads and avoid painful calf loading.

Key guardrails:
- If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade the session to easy. Do not try to “make up” missed intensity later in the week.
- Prioritize sleep and simple recovery habits to support load tolerance.
- Maintain daily calf/ankle mobility and keep runs very easy with short duration if energy feels limited.

Flexibility:
- Your week can shift by a few hours without changing the overall plan. If a session needs to move, aim to keep the same order and load.

Optional sign-off:
Let me know how it goes.

inbound.subject: First check-in after an easy run
inbound.body: Training check-in. Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 4. Pain score: 2 out of 10. Risk candidate is green. I did a run for 45m and 6 km yesterday. Felt good, energy ok, little sore, slept well. Missed sessions count: 0. I want to keep building carefully.

outbound.subject: Re: First check-in after an easy run
outbound.text:
Your check-in notes are clear: Achilles pain is about 2/10 after yesterday’s 6 km easy run, energy is okay, and you want a careful buildup. We’ll run four sessions this week (Mon/Wed/Fri/Sun) with a conservative load and clear guardrails to protect your Achilles while we rebuild fitness for the May half.
Mon — Easy aerobic 40–50 minutes
- Easy effort throughout, with a light warm-up and cooldown plus short mobility work
- Cadence light, posture tall
- Focus on staying comfortable and keeping load well below any painful ranges
Wed — Tempo 40–50 minutes total (15–25 minutes tempo)
- 10–15 minutes easy warm-up, 15–25 minutes controlled tempo, 5–10 minutes cooldown
- Tempo should feel controlled and steady, not forced
Fri — Easy aerobic 40–50 minutes
- Same approach as Monday: easy pace, mobility, and restraint with calf loading
Sun — Achilles-friendly strength 40–50 minutes
- Calf eccentrics, glute work, split squats, core, and ankle mobility
- Keep resistance moderate and movement smooth to protect the Achilles
Guidance and guardrails
- Training continues, but execution remains controlled and responsive to the week
- Hit the key sessions without adding extra load; don’t try to “make up” missed intensity later in the week
- If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade the session to easy
- Use the energy-management rule: cut sessions short if energy drops; avoid replacing missed intensity later
- Daily mobility, steady sleep, and simple recovery habits support the work
Open loop for this week
- Report any change in Achilles pain or tightness (morning stiffness, activity-related pain) so load can be adjusted immediately
You’re set to stay conservative and focused this week. If you want, you can share how the first session goes and I’ll help adjust next steps quickly.
outbound.html:
Your check-in notes are clear: Achilles pain is about 2/10 after yesterday’s 6 km easy run, energy is okay, and you want a careful buildup. We’ll run four sessions this week (Mon/Wed/Fri/Sun) with a conservative load and clear guardrails to protect your Achilles while we rebuild fitness for the May half.

Mon — Easy aerobic 40–50 minutes
- Easy effort throughout, with a light warm-up and cooldown plus short mobility work
- Cadence light, posture tall
- Focus on staying comfortable and keeping load well below any painful ranges

Wed — Tempo 40–50 minutes total (15–25 minutes tempo)
- 10–15 minutes easy warm-up, 15–25 minutes controlled tempo, 5–10 minutes cooldown
- Tempo should feel controlled and steady, not forced

Fri — Easy aerobic 40–50 minutes
- Same approach as Monday: easy pace, mobility, and restraint with calf loading

Sun — Achilles-friendly strength 40–50 minutes
- Calf eccentrics, glute work, split squats, core, and ankle mobility
- Keep resistance moderate and movement smooth to protect the Achilles

Guidance and guardrails
- Training continues, but execution remains controlled and responsive to the week
- Hit the key sessions without adding extra load; don’t try to “make up” missed intensity later in the week
- If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade the session to easy
- Use the energy-management rule: cut sessions short if energy drops; avoid replacing missed intensity later
- Daily mobility, steady sleep, and simple recovery habits support the work

Open loop for this week
- Report any change in Achilles pain or tightness (morning stiffness, activity-related pain) so load can be adjusted immediately

You’re set to stay conservative and focused this week. If you want, you can share how the first session goes and I’ll help adjust next steps quickly.
